×
Steak King HK · Evergreen Strategy · July 2026

De-risking the month

Ten trading days carry 57% of our revenue. This is the plan to move that weight onto the other twenty, using an always-on deal engine borrowed from All Fresh Seafood.

Built on 40,653 Shopify orders and 137 email campaigns · June 2025 to July 2026
57%of a month's revenue lands on just 10 trading days
37 daysmedian gap between a repeat customer's orders
2.5×repeat buyers over-index into sale windows
$1,083median BAU cart — $83 over the free-shipping line
$99kaverage BAU day vs $323k on a sale day
01The diagnosis

A BAU day is not an AOV problem

The instinct is to assume BAU days are weak because people spend less. They don't. They spend more.

BAU baskets are bigger than sale baskets

BAU AOV is $1,398. Sale-day AOV is $1,334. A sale does not make anyone spend more. It makes 3.6× more people buy: 77 orders on an average BAU day against 276 on a sale day.

So the lever we are pulling is order volume, not cart size. Every mechanic in this document is built to bring more people to checkout on an ordinary Tuesday, not to inflate the baskets of the people already there.

The $1,000 free-shipping line is what actually builds a cart

Delivery costs $75–85 below $1,000 and is free at or above it. The behaviour that follows is the single strongest pattern in the account:

Median BAU cart: $1,083  ·  free-shipping threshold: $1,000  ·  overshoot: +$83

30% of every BAU order lands in the $1,000–1,199 band. Customers build a cart until they clear free shipping, then they stop. Nobody is browsing. They are solving a shipping problem.

Where BAU carts land
Share of BAU orders by cart value. The cliff at $1,000 is the free-shipping threshold.
under $5006%
$500–80011%
$800–1,0005%
$1,000–1,20030%
$1,200–1,50017%
$1,500–2,00010%
$2,000–3,00017%
$3,000+4%
Only 5% of carts sit in the $800–1,000 band. Almost nobody stops just short of free shipping. They either clear it or they never got close.

The anatomy of a normal cart: one anchor, two fillers

3.4 lines, 4.5 units. The anchor is a chilled premium steak at $600–900. The fillers are frozen commodity proteins at a median of $178: whole Norwegian salmon, chicken thighs, Angus mince, burrata, salmon burgers.

The anchor is the decision. The fillers are just the arithmetic of getting to $1,000. That structure is the opportunity, and the whole strategy in this document is built on it.

02The dependency

The store runs on repeat buyers who buy once a month

And that single monthly purchase is not landing on a random day.

Three quarters of the money comes from people who have bought before

Across the last 13 months, 75% of revenue and 70% of orders come from customers who purchased more than once in that window. Just over half of all orders are not a customer's first.

Note: our Klaviyo returning-customer figure runs higher (~88%) because it counts lifetime history, while this window can only see 13 months and treats a long-standing customer's first order in the window as new. The direction is the same either way: this is a repeat-purchase business.

How often does a repeat customer actually buy?
Orders per month across their active span. 6,225 repeat customers.
under 0.5/mo29%
0.5–1.0/mo44%
1.0–1.5/mo17%
1.5–2.5/mo9%
2.5+/mo2%
Median gap between orders: 37 days. Median frequency: 0.69 orders a month. Only 16% of repeat customers buy more than about 1.3× a month. 73% buy less than once a month.

So the average Steak King customer buys roughly once every five weeks. They have one purchase in them per cycle. The only question that matters is which day they spend it on.

The answer, and the problem

Sale days are 18% of the calendar. But 46% of all repeat-customer orders land on one. That is a 2.5× over-index. It holds for acquisition too: 39% of first orders happen on a sale day, a 2.1× over-index.

And 13% of our repeat customers have never once bought outside a sale window.

Read plainly: we have trained the list to hold their one monthly purchase for Meat Emporium. That is why BAU days sag, and it is why a soft MES does not just miss its own target, it takes the month down with it. There is no second engine to catch it. In June the M9 heroes went out of stock mid-sale and there was nothing else running.

10 days out of 30 carry the month
Share of each month's revenue earned on its ten biggest trading days. This is not a growth artefact — it is stable across the year.
Sep 2548%
Oct 2552%
Nov 2574%
Dec 2549%
Jan 2655%
Feb 2659%
Mar 2656%
Apr 2657%
May 2665%
Jun 2658%
A third of the trading days make well over half the money. Every month, we bet the number on ten days and a handful of SKUs.
03The strategy

Front-load the month onto the evergreen days

Give the customer a reason to spend their monthly purchase on day 6 instead of day 24.

The proposal is to run a flash / LMT-style deal every day of the evergreen period, as a permanent fixture of the website rather than an email campaign. One deal a day, live for 24 hours, then replaced.

First, the proof that we cannot email our way out of this

We have been sending more and earning less, and the trend is not subtle. Since January, monthly email volume has grown 59% while revenue per recipient has fallen 54%. June was our heaviest sending month of the year and one of our worst earning ones.

We are sending more
Email recipients per month, all campaigns. 2026.
Jan608k
Feb593k
Mar683k
Apr815k
May734k
Jun964k
Jul*297k
25 campaigns in June, against 14 in May. *July is partial, to the 11th.
And earning less from every send
Revenue per recipient, HKD. Same months, same axis.
Jan$3.79
Feb$2.67
Mar$3.55
Apr$3.21
May$2.80
Jun$1.74
Jul*$1.23
Click rate tells the same story: 2.07% in January, 1.05% in June. The list is not responding less because the copy got worse. It is responding less because it is tired.

Read the two charts together

June: 964,000 recipients — the most all year — returning $1.74 per recipient, less than half of January's $3.79. We pushed 59% more volume through the list and pulled less total revenue out of it than we did in March on 30% fewer sends.

This is what a tired list looks like, and it is the ceiling on the current strategy. We cannot fix soft BAU days by sending more email, because sending more email is now actively costing us. The deal engine has to live somewhere else.

So it lives on the website, not in the inbox

Email supports it once or twice a week at most. That is not caution, it is what the data above demands. If this becomes a daily email programme we will drive revenue per recipient below $1 and walk into Meat Emporium with a list that has stopped listening.

On the site, the same deal does three jobs email cannot:

  • It converts the traffic we are already paying for. Every visitor from Meta and Google lands on a live offer instead of a full-price catalogue.
  • It teaches people to come back. A deal that changes daily is a reason to open the site on a Tuesday. That is the habit All Fresh has built, and it is the habit that fills BAU days.
  • It costs no sends. The deal runs whether or not we email, so we keep the list fresh for the sale.

WhatsApp carries the daily beat, since it is already our highest-spiking launch channel.

What we are copying from All Fresh Seafood

A US premium seafood brand we have run before. They share our profile almost exactly: high-ticket premium protein, delivery-based, promo-led. They run a deal every single day and it drives effectively all of their sales. Two things do the work.

The banner. Top of every page: one deal, a live countdown, one button. Directly under it sits the free-shipping threshold, so the two mechanics reinforce each other on every single pageview.
The deals page. A permanent home for the offers, 82 products deep. Their own copy: "check here frequently for pop-ups and flash sales." It is an appointment, and it is why customers come back without being emailed.

The long-run effect matters as much as the daily one: the products they put on deal become their best-sellers. The deal slot is how they manufacture demand for a SKU, not just how they discount one.

04The mechanic

The $800 rule

This is the part that de-risks the product line. Build every deal so that one product does $800 on its own.

Why $800

The median BAU cart is $1,083 against a $1,000 free-shipping line. People are trying to clear that line. So we build the deal so the deal product alone gets them to roughly $800, and the last $200 is closed by a single cheap SKU.

Deal product (2× qty of a mid-tier cut)  ~$800
+ one gap-closer (chicken breast, salmon, mince)  ~$150–250
= $950–1,050  free shipping cleared, cart complete, one decision made.

The customer adds one thing and they are done. We are not asking them to build a basket. We are handing them a cart that is already 80% finished.

And this is what breaks the hero dependency

The $800 deal product is never an M9 variant. It is drawn from the next tier down: the rank 5–30 sellers and the slow movers. The $200 gap is closed by a $150 chicken or salmon SKU, not by a hero either.

Which means: the deal engine keeps working when the M9 Ribeye and Striploin are out of stock. Today, when those two SKUs go, the month goes with them. Under this model, an OOS hero costs us a hero, not a month.

And because a deal SKU becomes a best-seller over time, every rotation is an audition. 808 of our 1,182 BAU SKUs sold under $5k in five months. That is the bench we are trying to promote from.

Deal candidates that hit ~$800 without a hero

Product (BAU rank)Unit priceDeal qtyDeal value
Chilled Wagyu M4-5 Tenderloin / Chateaubriand 800g (#5)$4882×$976
Chilled Rangers Station Black Angus M2-3 Ribeye 1kg (#6)$2983×$894
Chilled Rangers Station Wagyu M3-4 Ribeye 1kg (#7)$4882×$976
Chilled Organic Grass Fed Angus Tenderloin 1kg (#8)$3982×$796
Chilled Wagyu M4-5 T-Bone / Porterhouse 1kg (#17)$4382×$876
Chilled Wagyu M9+ Beef Cheeks 1kg (#21)$3982×$796
Chilled Black Angus M1-2 Ribeye 2kg (#28)$7881×$788
Frozen Duck Breasts 1kg (#30)$2983×$894
Chilled USDA Choice Ribeye 1kg (slow mover)$4482×$896
Frozen Seabass Fillets 4×180g (slow mover)$2783×$834

Prices are current BAU medians before any deal discount. The point is the structure: a 2× or 3× quantity play on almost any rank-5-to-30 cut lands naturally between $700 and $1,000. margin floor per tier to be confirmed — David

The gap-closers

These are the SKUs customers already reach for to clear free shipping, so they need no persuading. They sit ready as a one-click upsell on the deal page and in cart.

Gap-closer SKUPriceUnits sold (BAU, 5mo)
Frozen Whole Norwegian Salmon Fillet 1.2–1.8kg$1981,165
Frozen Barn Raised Chicken Thighs 1kg$168974
Frozen Black Angus Grass Fed Minced Beef 1kg$150644
Frozen Barn Raised Chicken Breast Fillets 1kg$148382
Frozen Tasmanian Salmon Burger 600g$159217
05The deals

The deal types

One deal a day, live 24 hours. Rotating SKUs, never a hero, never sitewide. Every one of them is built to land the deal product at roughly $800 on its own.

TIER 1 — RUN THESE FIRST

1. Buy One, Get One Free

Run it

The headline mechanic, and the one All Fresh leans on hardest. Unit-based rather than discount-based: the customer gets more meat, not a cheaper price, so the SKU's price integrity survives in a way a "50% off" banner does not.

2× Rangers Station M3-4 Ribeye @ $488 = $976  →  buy one, get one free. Customer receives 2kg, cart clears free shipping on the deal alone.

2. Quantity Flash — 2kg+

Run it

A deep price, unlocked only at 2kg or more. It builds the $800 basket by design and pushes real volume through one SKU, which is what turns a rank-20 product into a rank-5 product.

3× Black Angus M2-3 Ribeye @ $298 = $894  →  flash price on the 3kg tier only.

Freezer capacity is the real ceiling in Hong Kong flats. 2–3kg is the sweet spot; 5kg stalls.

3. Exclusive Bundle Drop

Run it

A fixed box at a fixed price, available for 24 hours only. It removes the decision entirely: the customer is not building a cart, they are buying an answer. King's Boxes already prove the model, and the Organic Ribeye × Striploin Set draws 60–70% new customers.

Boxes price above the free-shipping line by design, so they need no gap-closer at all. This is the acquisition slot.

TIER 2 — NEW MECHANICS TO TEST

4. Mix & Match — any 3 for $799

New

A curated wall of 8–10 cuts, all normally $280–350. Pick any three for a flat $799. The customer gets choice, which converts better than a single imposed SKU, and we get to decide exactly which products are on the wall.

This is the most efficient slow-mover clearer we have: put two strong cuts on the wall to draw people in, and six we need to move. It lands the $800 basket by construction, every single time, with zero maths on our side.

5. Butcher's Choice Box

New

A fixed weight at a fixed price, but we choose the cuts. "4kg of premium beef, $799, butcher's pick." The customer is buying the value and the surprise, not a specific SKU.

The strategic value here is enormous: it is the only deal that is immune to stock-outs. If the M9 lands short, the box simply contains something else. It also lets us clear whatever is actually sitting in the cold room, which no other mechanic does.

To be clear, this is a mystery box, not a mystery discount. The price is known. The contents are the surprise.

6. The Restaurant Cut

New

We own four restaurants under the same parent: Flat Iron HK, Picanhas HK, Punjab Warriors and Lasagna Factory. Nobody else in the market can run this deal.

Take the exact cut Flat Iron serves, put it on the site for 24 hours at a deal price, and sell it as "the steak they serve on Wyndham Street, on your table tonight." It is a deal with a story, it borrows credibility we already own, and it makes the cut feel chosen rather than discounted.

7. Cut of the Week

New

Not a 24-hour flash. One unfamiliar cut, held at a deal price for seven days, with Johnny cooking it on camera behind it. A campaign for a single SKU rather than a discount on one.

This is the slow half of the de-risking play. 808 of our 1,182 BAU SKUs sold under $5k in five months. A cut only becomes a best-seller after enough people have cooked it once. Picanha, launching now, is the obvious first candidate.

8. The Last Crate

New

A fixed unit count at a deep price, with a live counter on the page. "80 crates. When they're gone, they're gone." A counter beats a timer, because it is honestly true.

Point it at short-dated or over-ordered stock and it turns an inventory problem into a demand event instead of a markdown. The mechanic pays for itself twice.

TIER 3 — BLOCKED ON TOOLING

9. Beat the Clock

TBD

Price starts low and climbs every two hours until midnight. It worked as a cart-value taper during the USDA flash and it is the strongest urgency mechanic available to us.

It needs tooling we do not have. Doing it properly requires an app that auto-applies a discount by threshold and time. Without one we are hand-setting coupon codes through the evening, which is not workable as a recurring fixture. scope an auto-discount app before committing

What we are deliberately not doing

Mystery Discount is not in this programme. It works, but it is a reactivation lever aimed at people who already know us, and roughly 90% of its revenue comes from repeat customers. Its power comes from being rare. Spending it on an ordinary Tuesday wastes it. It belongs inside Meat Emporium as an LMT, and that is where it stays.

This list is a starting point, not a finished menu. The engine is the point: a deal slot that has to be filled every day will force us to keep inventing mechanics, and the ones that work will make themselves obvious quickly.

06The timing

When the deal goes live

BAU orders by hour, 328 days. There are two peaks, and we only need one of them.

BAU orders by hour
Hong Kong local time. 20,000+ BAU orders.
08:00637
09:001050
10:001475
11:001775
12:001758
13:001777
14:001630
15:001617
16:001541
17:001647
18:001123
19:001029
20:001042
21:001084
22:001002
23:001007
00:00618
AM peak · 10:00–16:00 · the deal window PM plateau · 17:00–00:00 · the expiry window

Go live at 10:00. The deal launches into the ramp, peaks with the 11:00–13:00 lunch window (our single biggest ordering hour of the day), and holds through the afternoon.

Expire at midnight. The PM plateau from 19:00 to 23:00 never really dies, and a countdown that runs out at midnight converts it. One deal, one day, two natural bites at it.

We are deliberately not running an AM and a PM deal. One deal a day is enough to build the habit and it halves the operational load. If the proof of concept works, a second daily slot is the obvious next test.

07The consequence

What this does to Meat Emporium

If BAU carries more of the month, the sale gets to be a sale again.

Shorter, sharper, rarer

Right now MES runs ten days because it has to carry the month. If the evergreen engine works, it does not. The sale shortens to seven days and stops being a slow bleed of discounts that customers can afford to wait out.

The heroes go back behind the sale

The M9 Ribeye and Striploin stop appearing in BAU deals entirely. They become the reason to show up to Meat Emporium, not the thing you can get cheap on a Tuesday. That also means we stop discounting the two SKUs most likely to sell out anyway.

The loudest mechanics get reserved

Mystery Discount and the deepest LMTs move inside the sale window, where a repeat-order spike is worth the most. BAU keeps the quieter mechanics: BOGO, quantity flashes, bundle drops.

And the forecast stops being a single bet

July is already reforecast from $3.8M to $3.5M because MES is going in with a lean product list and no M9 variants. That is the exact failure this fixes. Lifting BAU days from $99k to $130k across the evergreen window is roughly $600k a month, and it arrives before the sale rather than depending on it.

The test: two or three deals, not eleven

We are not proposing we switch on a full daily engine for the whole evergreen window and hope. That is a lot of discounted volume to commit before we know the mechanic works on this list.

Instead: run two to three flash deals in the run-up to Meat Emporium, spaced across the evergreen days, each using a different mechanic. It is enough to read the uplift on a BAU day, cheap enough to be wrong, and fast enough to learn before the sale.

What we are measuring on each deal day, against a normal BAU day of 77 orders / $99k:

  • Orders, not AOV. Did the deal day beat 77 orders? That is the whole thesis.
  • Did the cart complete itself? We want to see carts landing at $950–1,100 with the deal product plus one gap-closer. If people are buying the deal product alone and paying shipping, the $800 rule is mispriced.
  • Hero-free orders. The share of deal-day orders containing no M9. This is the de-risking metric and the reason we are doing any of this.
  • Did we borrow or create demand? Check the days either side. A deal day that simply pulls forward Thursday's orders is not an uplift, it is a rearrangement.

If two or three deals move a BAU day meaningfully above $99k without denting the days around them, we scale to the daily engine and shorten Meat Emporium. If they do not, we have spent very little finding out.

08Scoping

How the SKUs actually work

This is the part that decides whether the engine is buildable or a weekly fire drill.

Most deals need their own product, not just a discount

A "3kg for $894" flash is not the 1kg SKU with a coupon on it. It is a different unit of sale. The same is true of BOGO, Mix & Match and the Butcher's Box. Each of those needs its own product record and its own PDP, with the deal price, the deal photography, and a countdown on the page.

We already do exactly this for Meat Emporium, where products are cloned with sale-specific titles. The daily engine is the same pattern, running more often and on a tighter turnaround.

What has to be decided before day one

  • Cloned SKU vs. variant vs. automatic discount. Cloned SKUs give us clean PDPs and clean reporting, but they fragment inventory and they are how heroes silently went out of stock in June. A variant on the parent SKU keeps one stock pool. This needs a call from David, per mechanic.
  • Ring-fenced inventory. Every deal needs an allocation. A deal that sells out at 11am is a bad day, not a good one, and a deal that eats the stock we were holding for Meat Emporium is worse.
  • A naming convention, so reporting can separate deal revenue from BAU revenue cleanly. Without it we will not be able to answer whether this worked.
  • An OOS notification to the ad team. A King's Box was pulled silently once and broke live ads. On a daily rotation that risk multiplies.
  • Who builds it, daily. A deal a day is a real operational load: a PDP, a banner, a WhatsApp send, and a takedown. That is a named owner, not a spare hour.
09Requirements & risks

What we need, and how this goes wrong

To build it

  • A site deal bar with a live countdown, clicking through to the deal product. Directly above or below the free-shipping message, the way All Fresh stacks them. Without this the engine has no home.
  • A permanent /deals page that always has something on it, so "there's always a deal on" is a claim the site can back up.
  • Gap-closer upsells surfaced in cart when the basket sits between $800 and $1,000.
  • An auto-discount app if we want Beat the Clock. Otherwise it stays out of the rotation.
  • Margin floors per product tier from David before the rota is locked.

The four ways it fails

  • It becomes an email programme. Two sends a week, maximum. The habit we are building is check the site, not open the email. If we mail this daily we will burn the list before Meat Emporium and repeat the June mistake exactly.
  • We deal a hero. The moment an M9 goes on a daily deal, we have taught the list that the best cut gets cheap on an ordinary day, and we have discounted the SKU most likely to sell out at full price anyway. Rotating SKUs is precisely what lets All Fresh run a deal every day without training people to wait: the deal is always on, but never on the one thing you specifically came for.
  • It cannibalises the sale. BAU deal depth must stay clearly below MES depth. If a Tuesday ever beats Meat Emporium on price, we have dismantled our own flagship to fill a Tuesday.
  • We run it blind on margin. This plan has no COGS behind it, which is exactly why we are testing two or three deals rather than committing to thirty. Margin floors first, calendar second.
Steak King HK · De-risking the Month · July 2026 · Tethrd
Source: 40,653 Shopify orders, 8 June 2025 to 14 July 2026. "Sale day" = any day above 1.9× median daily revenue (74 of 402 days). Cart, filler and product figures from the 150-day BAU window (Feb–Jul 2026). All figures HKD.
